question:
What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained in a 4-dimensional
hypercube of side length 1?
solution:
The largest possible radius is $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
It will be convenient to solve
the problem for a hypercube of side length 2 instead, in which case
we are trying to show that the largest radius is $\sqrt{2}$.

Choose coordinates so that the interior of the hypercube
is the set $H = [-1,1]^4$ in $\RR^4$. Let $C$ be a circle
centered at the point $P$. Then $C$ is contained both in $H$
and its reflection across $P$; these intersect in a rectangular
paralellepiped each of whose pairs of opposite faces are at most
2 unit apart. Consequently, if we translate $C$ so that its center
moves to the point $O = (0,0,0,0)$ at the center of $H$,
then it remains entirely inside $H$.

This means that the answer we seek equals the largest possible radius
of a circle $C$ contained in $H$ \emph{and centered at $O$}.
Let $v_1 = (v_{11}, \dots, v_{14})$ and $v_2 = (v_{21},\dots,v_{24})$
be two points on $C$ lying on radii perpendicular to each other.
Then the points of the circle can be expressed as
$v_1 \cos \theta + v_2 \sin \theta$ for $0 \leq \theta < 2\pi$.
Then $C$ lies in $H$ if and only if for each $i$, we have
\[
|v_{1i} \cos \theta + v_{2i} \sin \theta|
\leq 1 \qquad (0 \leq \theta < 2\pi).
\]
In geometric terms, the vector $(v_{1i}, v_{2i})$ in $\RR^2$
has dot product at most 1 with every unit vector. Since this holds
for the unit vector in the same direction as
$(v_{1i}, v_{2i})$, we must have
\[
v_{1i}^2 + v_{2i}^2 \leq 1 \qquad (i=1,\dots,4).
\]
Conversely, if this holds, then the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
and the above analysis imply that $C$ lies in $H$.

If $r$ is the radius of $C$, then
\begin{align*}
2 r^2 &= \sum_{i=1}^4 v_{1i}^2 + \sum_{i=1}^4 v_{2i}^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^4 (v_{1i}^2 + v_{2i}^2) \\
&\leq 4,
\end{align*}
so $r \leq \sqrt{2}$.
Since this is achieved by the circle
through $(1,1,0,0)$ and $(0,0,1,1)$,
it is the desired maximum.

\textbf{Remark:}
One may similarly ask for the radius of the largest $k$-dimensional
ball inside an $n$-dimensional unit hypercube; the given problem is
the case $(n,k) = (4,2)$.
Daniel Kane gives the following argument to show that the maximum radius
in this case is $\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{n}{k}}$.
(Thanks for Noam Elkies for passing this along.)

We again scale up by a factor of 2, so that we are trying to show that
the maximum radius $r$ of a $k$-dimensional ball contained in the hypercube
$[-1,1]^n$ is $\sqrt{\frac{n}{k}}$. Again, there is no loss of generality
in centering the ball at the origin. Let $T: \RR^k \to \RR^n$ be a
similitude carrying the unit ball to this embedded $k$-ball.
Then there exists a vector $v_i \in \RR^k$ such that
for $e_1,\dots,e_n$ the standard basis of $\RR^n$,
$x \cdot v_i = T(x) \cdot e_i$ for all $x \in \RR^k$.
The condition of the problem is equivalent to requiring
$|v_i| \leq 1$ for all $i$, while the radius $r$ of the embedded ball
is determined by the fact that for all $x \in \RR^k$,
\[
r^2 (x \cdot x) = T(x) \cdot T(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n x \cdot v_i.
\]
Let $M$ be the matrix with columns $v_1,\dots,v_k$; then $MM^T = r^2 I_k$,
for $I_k$ the $k \times k$ identity matrix. We then have
\begin{align*}
kr^2 &= \Trace(r^2 I_k) = \Trace(MM^T)\\
&= \Trace(M^TM) = \sum_{i=1}^n |v_i|^2 \\
&\leq n,
\end{align*}
yielding the upper bound $r \leq \sqrt{\frac{n}{k}}$.

To show that this bound is optimal, it is enough to show that one can
find an orthogonal projection of $\RR^n$ onto $\RR^k$ so that the
projections of the $e_i$ all have the same norm (one can then rescale
to get the desired configuration of  $v_1,\dots,v_n$). We construct
such a configuration by a ``smoothing'' argument. Startw with any
projection.
Let $w_1,\dots,w_n$ be the projections of $e_1,\dots,e_n$.
If the desired condition is not
achieved, we can choose $i,j$ such that
\[
|w_i|^2 < \frac{1}{n} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_n|^2) < |w_j|^2.
\]
By precomposing
with a suitable rotation that fixes $e_h$ for $h \neq i,j$,
we can vary $|w_i|, |w_j|$ without varying $|w_i|^2 + |w_j|^2$
or $|w_h|$ for $h \neq i,j$. We can thus choose such a rotation to
force one of $|w_i|^2, |w_j|^2$ to become equal to
$\frac{1}{n} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_n|^2)$.
Repeating at most $n-1$ times gives the desired configuration.

descriptive_long.question:
What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained in a 4-dimensional
hypercube of side length 1?
descriptive_long.solution:
The largest possible radius is $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. It will be convenient to solve
 the problem for a hypercube of side length 2 instead, in which case
 we are trying to show that the largest radius is $\sqrt{2}$.

Choose coordinates so that the interior of the hypercube
is the set $hypercube = [-1,1]^4$ in $\RR^4$. Let $circle$ be a circle
centered at the point $centroid$. Then $circle$ is contained both in $hypercube$
and its reflection across $centroid$; these intersect in a rectangular
paralellepiped each of whose pairs of opposite faces are at most
2 unit apart. Consequently, if we translate $circle$ so that its center
moves to the point $originpoint = (0,0,0,0)$ at the center of $hypercube$,
then it remains entirely inside $hypercube$.

This means that the answer we seek equals the largest possible radius
of a circle $circle$ contained in $hypercube$ \emph{and centered at $originpoint$}.
Let $radiusvecone = (veconeone, \dots, veconefour)$ and $radiusvectwo = (vectwoone,\dots,vectwofour)$
be two points on $circle$ lying on radii perpendicular to each other.
Then the points of the circle can be expressed as
$radiusvecone \cos \theta + radiusvectwo \sin \theta$ for $0 \leq \theta < 2\pi$.
Then $circle$ lies in $hypercube$ if and only if for each $indexi$, we have
\[
|veconecomp \cos \theta + vectwocomp \sin \theta|
\leq 1 \qquad (0 \leq \theta < 2\pi).
\]
In geometric terms, the vector $(veconecomp, vectwocomp)$ in $\RR^2$
has dot product at most 1 with every unit vector. Since this holds
for the unit vector in the same direction as
$(veconecomp, vectwocomp)$, we must have
\[
veconecomp^2 + vectwocomp^2 \leq 1 \qquad (indexi=1,\dots,4).
\]
Conversely, if this holds, then the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
and the above analysis imply that $circle$ lies in $hypercube$.

If $radius$ is the radius of $circle$, then
\begin{align*}
2 radius^2 &= \sum_{indexi=1}^4 veconecomp^2 + \sum_{indexi=1}^4 vectwocomp^2 \\
&= \sum_{indexi=1}^4 (veconecomp^2 + vectwocomp^2) \\
&\leq 4,
\end{align*}
so $radius \leq \sqrt{2}$.
Since this is achieved by the circle
through $(1,1,0,0)$ and $(0,0,1,1)$,
it is the desired maximum.

\textbf{Remark:}
One may similarly ask for the radius of the largest indexk-dimensional
ball inside an dimensionn-dimensional unit hypercube; the given problem is
the case $(dimensionn,indexk) = (4,2)$.
Daniel Kane gives the following argument to show that the maximum radius
in this case is $\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{dimensionn}{indexk}}$.
(Thanks for Noam Elkies for passing this along.)

We again scale up by a factor of 2, so that we are trying to show that
the maximum radius $radius$ of a indexk-dimensional ball contained in the hypercube
$[-1,1]^{dimensionn}$ is $\sqrt{\frac{dimensionn}{indexk}}$. Again, there is no loss of generality
in centering the ball at the origin. Let $transform: \RR^{indexk} \to \RR^{dimensionn}$ be a
similitude carrying the unit ball to this embedded indexk-ball.
Then there exists a vector $v_i \in \RR^{indexk}$ such that
for $e_1,\dots,e_{dimensionn}$ the standard basis of $\RR^{dimensionn}$,
$pointx \cdot v_i = transform(pointx) \cdot basisvectori$ for all $pointx \in \RR^{indexk}$.
The condition of the problem is equivalent to requiring
$|v_i| \leq 1$ for all $indexi$, while the radius $radius$ of the embedded ball
is determined by the fact that for all $pointx \in \RR^{indexk}$,
\[
radius^2 (pointx \cdot pointx) = transform(pointx) \cdot transform(pointx) = \sum_{indexi=1}^{dimensionn} pointx \cdot v_i.
\]
Let $matrixm$ be the matrix with columns $radiusvecone,\dots,v_{indexk}$; then $matrixm matrixm^T = radius^2 identityk$,
for $identityk$ the indexk \times indexk identity matrix. We then have
\begin{align*}
indexk radius^2 &= \Trace(radius^2 identityk) = \Trace(matrixm matrixm^T)\\
&= \Trace(matrixm^T matrixm) = \sum_{indexi=1}^{dimensionn} |v_i|^2 \\
&\leq dimensionn,
\end{align*}
yielding the upper bound $radius \leq \sqrt{\frac{dimensionn}{indexk}}$.

To show that this bound is optimal, it is enough to show that one can
find an orthogonal projection of $\RR^{dimensionn}$ onto $\RR^{indexk}$ so that the
projections of the basisvectori all have the same norm (one can then rescale
to get the desired configuration of  $radiusvecone,\dots,v_{dimensionn}$). We construct
such a configuration by a ``smoothing'' argument. Startw with any
projection.
Let $w_1,\dots,w_{dimensionn}$ be the projections of $e_1,\dots,e_{dimensionn}$.
If the desired condition is not
achieved, we can choose indexi,indexj such that
\[
|projvectori|^2 < \frac{1}{dimensionn} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{dimensionn}|^2) < |projvectorj|^2.
\]
By precomposing
with a suitable rotation that fixes $e_h$ for $h \neq indexi,indexj$,
we can vary $|projvectori|, |projvectorj|$ without varying $|projvectori|^2 + |projvectorj|^2$
or $|projvectorh|$ for $h \neq indexi,indexj$. We can thus choose such a rotation to
force one of $|projvectori|^2, |projvectorj|^2$ to become equal to
$\frac{1}{dimensionn} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{dimensionn}|^2)$.
Repeating at most dimensionn-1 times gives the desired configuration.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained in a 4-dimensional
hypercube of side length 1?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The largest possible radius is $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.  
It will be convenient to solve the problem for a hypercube of side length 2 instead, in which case we are trying to show that the largest radius is $\sqrt{2}$.  

Choose coordinates so that the interior of the hypercube is the set $neckbeard = [-1,1]^4$ in $\RR^4$.  Let $windstorm$ be a circle centered at the point $toadflax$.  Then $windstorm$ is contained both in $neckbeard$ and its reflection across $toadflax$; these intersect in a rectangular paralellepiped each of whose pairs of opposite faces are at most 2 unit apart.  Consequently, if we translate $windstorm$ so that its center moves to the point $lighthouse = (0,0,0,0)$ at the center of $neckbeard$, then it remains entirely inside $neckbeard$.  

This means that the answer we seek equals the largest possible radius of a circle $windstorm$ contained in $neckbeard$ \emph{and centered at $lighthouse$}.  Let $barrelful = (dragonfly, \dots, goldcrest)$ and $blackbird = (brickwork,\dots,smalltalk)$ be two points on $windstorm$ lying on radii perpendicular to each other.  Then the points of the circle can be expressed as $barrelful \cos \theta + blackbird \sin \theta$ for $0 \leq \theta < 2\pi$.  Then $windstorm$ lies in $neckbeard$ if and only if for each $conductor$, we have
\[
|shoelaces \cos \theta + thumbtack \sin \theta| \leq 1 \qquad (0 \leq \theta < 2\pi).
\]
In geometric terms, the vector $(shoelaces, thumbtack)$ in $\RR^2$ has dot product at most 1 with every unit vector.  Since this holds for the unit vector in the same direction as $(shoelaces, thumbtack)$, we must have
\[
shoelaces^2 + thumbtack^2 \leq 1 \qquad (conductor=1,\dots,4).
\]
Conversely, if this holds, then the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and the above analysis imply that $windstorm$ lies in $neckbeard$.  

If $marmalade$ is the radius of $windstorm$, then
\begin{align*}
2\,marmalade^2 &= \sum_{conductor=1}^{4} shoelaces^2 + \sum_{conductor=1}^{4} thumbtack^2\\
&= \sum_{conductor=1}^{4} (shoelaces^2 + thumbtack^2) \\
&\le 4,
\end{align*}
so $marmalade \le \sqrt{2}$.  Since this is achieved by the circle through $(1,1,0,0)$ and $(0,0,1,1)$, it is the desired maximum.  

\textbf{Remark:}  One may similarly ask for the radius of the largest $grassland$-dimensional ball inside an $arrowhead$-dimensional unit hypercube; the given problem is the case $(arrowhead,grassland) = (4,2)$. Daniel Kane gives the following argument to show that the maximum radius in this case is $\frac12\sqrt{\frac{arrowhead}{grassland}}$. (Thanks to Noam Elkies for passing this along.)  

We again scale up by a factor of 2, so that we are trying to show that the maximum radius $marmalade$ of a $grassland$-dimensional ball contained in the hypercube $[-1,1]^{arrowhead}$ is $\sqrt{\frac{arrowhead}{grassland}}$. Again, there is no loss of generality in centering the ball at the origin.  Let $snowdrift:\RR^{grassland}\to\RR^{arrowhead}$ be a similitude carrying the unit ball to this embedded $grassland$-ball.  Then there exists a vector $v_{\text{conductor}}\in\RR^{grassland}$ such that for $dreamland_1,\dots,dreamland_{arrowhead}$ the standard basis of $\RR^{arrowhead}$,
$cornflakes\cdot v_{\text{conductor}} = snowdrift(cornflakes)\cdot dreamland$ for all $cornflakes\in\RR^{grassland}$.  The condition of the problem is equivalent to requiring $|v_{\text{conductor}}| \le 1$ for all $conductor$, while the radius $marmalade$ of the embedded ball is determined by the fact that for all $cornflakes \in \RR^{grassland}$,
\[
marmalade^2 (cornflakes \cdot cornflakes) = snowdrift(cornflakes) \cdot snowdrift(cornflakes) = \sum_{conductor=1}^{arrowhead} cornflakes \cdot v_{\text{conductor}}.
\]
Let $houseplant$ be the matrix with columns $barrelful,\dots,v_{grassland}$; then $houseplant houseplant^T = marmalade^2\,parchment$, for $parchment$ the $grassland \times grassland$ identity matrix.  We then have
\begin{align*}
grassland\,marmalade^2 &= \Trace(marmalade^2\,parchment) = \Trace(houseplant houseplant^T)\\
&= \Trace(houseplant^T houseplant) = \sum_{conductor=1}^{arrowhead} |v_{\text{conductor}}|^2 \\
&\le arrowhead,
\end{align*}
yielding the upper bound $marmalade \le \sqrt{\frac{arrowhead}{grassland}}$.  

To show that this bound is optimal, it is enough to show that one can find an orthogonal projection of $\RR^{arrowhead}$ onto $\RR^{grassland}$ so that the projections of the $dreamland_{\text{conductor}}$ all have the same norm (one can then rescale to get the desired configuration of $barrelful,\dots,v_{arrowhead}$). We construct such a configuration by a ``smoothing'' argument.  Start with any projection.  Let $w_1,\dots,w_{arrowhead}$ be the projections of $e_1,\dots,e_{arrowhead}$.  If the desired condition is not achieved, we can choose $conductor,rainstorm$ such that
\[
|hatchling|^2 < \frac1{arrowhead} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{arrowhead}|^2) < |floodgate|^2.
\]
By precomposing with a suitable rotation that fixes $e_h$ for $h \neq conductor,rainstorm$, we can vary $|hatchling|, |floodgate|$ without varying $|hatchling|^2 + |floodgate|^2$ or $|springbok|$ for $h \neq conductor,rainstorm$. We can thus choose such a rotation to force one of $|hatchling|^2, |floodgate|^2$ to become equal to $\frac1{arrowhead} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{arrowhead}|^2)$. Repeating at most $arrowhead-1$ times gives the desired configuration.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: What is the largest possible radius of a circle contained in a 4-dimensional hypercube of side length 1?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The largest possible radius is $ rac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
It will be convenient to solve
the problem for a hypercube of side length 2 instead, in which case
we are trying to show that the largest radius is $\sqrt{2}$.

Choose coordinates so that the interior of the hypercube
is the set $hypersphere = [-1,1]^4$ in $\RR^4$. Let $squareloop$ be a circle
centered at the point $vastplane$. Then $squareloop$ is contained both in $hypersphere$
and its reflection across $vastplane$; these intersect in a rectangular
paralellepiped each of whose pairs of opposite faces are at most
2 unit apart. Consequently, if we translate $squareloop$ so that its center
moves to the point $infinitypoint = (0,0,0,0)$ at the center of $hypersphere$,
then it remains entirely inside $hypersphere$.

This means that the answer we seek equals the largest possible radius
of a circle $squareloop$ contained in $hypersphere$ \emph{and centered at $infinitypoint$}.
Let $scalarnumber = (collectiveq, \dots, wholenessb)$ and $constantvalue = (fragmented,\dots,componentz)$
be two points on $squareloop$ lying on radii perpendicular to each other.
Then the points of the circle can be expressed as
$scalarnumber \cos 	heta + constantvalue \sin 	heta$ for $0 \leq 	heta < 2\pi$.
Then $squareloop$ lies in $hypersphere$ if and only if for each $totalindex$, we have
\[
|overallfactor \cos 	heta + singularterm \sin 	heta|
\leq 1 \qquad (0 \leq 	heta < 2\pi).
\]
In geometric terms, the vector $(overallfactor, singularterm)$ in $\RR^2$
has dot product at most 1 with every unit vector. Since this holds
for the unit vector in the same direction as
$(overallfactor, singularterm)$, we must have
\[
overallfactor^2 + singularterm^2 \leq 1 \qquad (totalindex=1,\dots,4).
\]
Conversely, if this holds, then the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
and the above analysis imply that $squareloop$ lies in $hypersphere$.

If $cornerlength$ is the radius of $squareloop$, then
 egin{align*}
2 cornerlength^2 &= \sum_{totalindex=1}^4 overallfactor^2 + \sum_{totalindex=1}^4 singularterm^2 \n&= \sum_{totalindex=1}^4 (overallfactor^2 + singularterm^2) \n&\leq 4,
\end{align*}
so $cornerlength \leq \sqrt{2}$.
Since this is achieved by the circle
through $(1,1,0,0)$ and $(0,0,1,1)$,
it is the desired maximum.

	extbf{Remark:}
One may similarly ask for the radius of the largest $voidness$-dimensional
ball inside an $mindimnn$-dimensional unit hypercube; the given problem is
the case $(mindimnn,voidness) = (4,2)$.
Daniel Kane gives the following argument to show that the maximum radius
in this case is $ rac{1}{2} \sqrt{ rac{mindimnn}{voidness}}$.
(Thanks for Noam Elkies for passing this along.)

We again scale up by a factor of 2, so that we are trying to show that
the maximum radius $cornerlength$ of a $voidness$-dimensional ball contained in the hypercube
$[-1,1]^{mindimnn}$ is $\sqrt{ rac{mindimnn}{voidness}}$. Again, there is no loss of generality
in centering the ball at the origin. Let $stagnation: \RR^{voidness} 	o \RR^{mindimnn}$ be a
similitude carrying the unit ball to this embedded $voidness$-ball.
Then there exists a vector $overallfactor_{totalindex} \in \RR^{voidness}$ such that
for $randomvector_1,\dots,randomvector_{mindimnn}$ the standard basis of $\RR^{mindimnn}$,
$permanent \cdot overallfactor_{totalindex} = stagnation(permanent) \cdot randomvector_{totalindex}$ for all $permanent \in \RR^{voidness}$.
The condition of the problem is equivalent to requiring
$|overallfactor_{totalindex}| \leq 1$ for all $totalindex$, while the radius $cornerlength$ of the embedded ball
is determined by the fact that for all $permanent \in \RR^{voidness}$,
\[
cornerlength^2 (permanent \cdot permanent) = stagnation(permanent) \cdot stagnation(permanent) = \sum_{totalindex=1}^{mindimnn} permanent \cdot overallfactor_{totalindex}.
\]
Let $singularity$ be the matrix with columns $overallfactor_1,\dots,overallfactor_{voidness}$; then $singularity singularity^{stagnation} = cornerlength^2 zeroarray$,
for $zeroarray$ the $voidness 	imes voidness$ identity matrix. We then have
 egin{align*}
voidness\,cornerlength^2 &= \Trace(cornerlength^2 zeroarray) = \Trace(singularity singularity^{stagnation})\n&= \Trace(singularity^{stagnation} singularity) = \sum_{totalindex=1}^{mindimnn} |overallfactor_{totalindex}|^2 \n&\leq mindimnn,
\end{align*}
yielding the upper bound $cornerlength \leq \sqrt{ rac{mindimnn}{voidness}}$.

To show that this bound is optimal, it is enough to show that one can
find an orthogonal projection of $\RR^{mindimnn}$ onto $\RR^{voidness}$ so that the
projections of the $randomvector_{totalindex}$ all have the same norm (one can then rescale
to get the desired configuration of  $overallfactor_1,\dots,overallfactor_{mindimnn}$). We construct
such a configuration by a ``smoothing'' argument. Startw with any
projection.
Let $w_1,\dots,w_{mindimnn}$ be the projections of $randomvector_1,\dots,randomvector_{mindimnn}$.
If the desired condition is not
achieved, we can choose $totalindex,combinedex$ such that
\[
|expansionone|^2 <  rac{1}{mindimnn} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{mindimnn}|^2) < |expansiontwo|^2.
\]
By precomposing
with a suitable rotation that fixes $randomvector_h$ for $h 
eq totalindex,combinedex$,
we can vary $|expansionone|, |expansiontwo|$ without varying $|expansionone|^2 + |expansiontwo|^2$
or $|expansionthr|$ for $h 
eq totalindex,combinedex$. We can thus choose such a rotation to
force one of $|expansionone|^2, |expansiontwo|^2$ to become equal to
$ rac{1}{mindimnn} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{mindimnn}|^2)$.
Repeating at most $mindimnn-1$ times gives the desired configuration.

garbled_string.solution:
The largest possible radius is $ rac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
It will be convenient to solve
the problem for a hypercube of side length 2 instead, in which case
we are trying to show that the largest radius is $\sqrt{2}$.

Choose coordinates so that the interior of the hypercube
is the set $qbvridyl = [-1,1]^4$ in $\RR^4$. Let $xazmteuv$ be a circle
centered at the point $gwouxnse$. Then $xazmteuv$ is contained both in $qbvridyl$
and its reflection across $gwouxnse$; these intersect in a rectangular
paralellepiped each of whose pairs of opposite faces are at most
2 unit apart. Consequently, if we translate $xazmteuv$ so that its center

of a circle $xazmteuv$ contained in $qbvridyl$ \emph{and centered at $kpryldav$}.

be two points on $xazmteuv$ lying on radii perpendicular to each other.
Then the points of the circle can be expressed as
$bhqwnsri \cos 	heta + nzxofuel \sin 	heta$ for $0 \leq 	heta < 2\pi$.
Then $xazmteuv$ lies in $qbvridyl$ if and only if for each $prxgmahu$, we have
\[
|bhqwnsrii \cos 	heta + nzxofueli \sin 	heta|
\leq 1 \qquad (0 \leq 	heta < 2\pi).
\]
In geometric terms, the vector $(bhqwnsrii, nzxofueli)$ in $\RR^2$
has dot product at most 1 with every unit vector. Since this holds
for the unit vector in the same direction as
$(bhqwnsrii, nzxofueli)$, we must have

If $lonqjpfk$ is the radius of $xazmteuv$, then
 egin{align*}
2 lonqjpfk^2 &= \sum_{prxgmahu=1}^4 bhqwnsrii^2 + \sum_{prxgmahu=1}^4 nzxofueli^2 \n&= \sum_{prxgmahu=1}^4 (bhqwnsrii^2 + nzxofueli^2) \n&\leq 4,

Since this is achieved by the circle

	extbf{Remark:}
One may similarly ask for the radius of the largest $slcfzqbn$-dimensional
ball inside an $yvkatmeg$-dimensional unit hypercube; the given problem is
the case $(yvkatmeg,slcfzqbn) = (4,2)$.
Daniel Kane gives the following argument to show that the maximum radius
in this case is $ rac{1}{2} \sqrt{ rac{yvkatmeg}{slcfzqbn}}$.
(Thanks for Noam Elkies for passing this along.)

We again scale up by a factor of 2, so that we are trying to show that
the maximum radius $lonqjpfk$ of a $slcfzqbn$-dimensional ball contained in the hypercube
$[-1,1]^{yvkatmeg}$ is $\sqrt{ rac{yvkatmeg}{slcfzqbn}}$. Again, there is no loss of generality
in centering the ball at the origin. Let $ugprhcei: \RR^{slcfzqbn} 	o \RR^{yvkatmeg}$ be a
similitude carrying the unit ball to this embedded $slcfzqbn$-ball.
Then there exists a vector $v_{prxgmahu} \in \RR^{slcfzqbn}$ such that
for $chzparwl_1,\dots,chzparwl_{yvkatmeg}$ the standard basis of $\RR^{yvkatmeg}$,
$tldwqzsa \cdot v_{prxgmahu} = ugprhcei(tldwqzsa) \cdot chzparwl$ for all $tldwqzsa \in \RR^{slcfzqbn}$.
The condition of the problem is equivalent to requiring
$|v_{prxgmahu}| \leq 1$ for all $prxgmahu$, while the radius $lonqjpfk$ of the embedded ball
is determined by the fact that for all $tldwqzsa \in \RR^{slcfzqbn}$,
\[
lonqjpfk^2 (tldwqzsa \cdot tldwqzsa) = ugprhcei(tldwqzsa) \cdot ugprhcei(tldwqzsa) = \sum_{prxgmahu=1}^{yvkatmeg} tldwqzsa \cdot v_{prxgmahu}.
\]
Let $jyshncvk$ be the matrix with columns $bhqwnsri,\dots,v_{slcfzqbn}$; then $jyshncvk jyshncvk^T = lonqjpfk^2 ftrbeimq$,
for $ftrbeimq$ the $slcfzqbn 	imes slcfzqbn$ identity matrix. We then have
 egin{align*}
slcfzqbn lonqjpfk^2 &= \Trace(lonqjpfk^2 ftrbeimq) = \Trace(jyshncvk jyshncvk^T)\
&= \Trace(jyshncvk^T jyshncvk) = \sum_{prxgmahu=1}^{yvkatmeg} |v_{prxgmahu}|^2 \n&\leq yvkatmeg,
\end{align*}
yielding the upper bound $lonqjpfk \leq \sqrt{ rac{yvkatmeg}{slcfzqbn}}$.

To show that this bound is optimal, it is enough to show that one can
find an orthogonal projection of $\RR^{yvkatmeg}$ onto $\RR^{slcfzqbn}$ so that the
projections of the $chzparwl_{prxgmahu}$ all have the same norm (one can then rescale
to get the desired configuration of  $bhqwnsri,\dots,v_{yvkatmeg}$). We construct
such a configuration by a ``smoothing'' argument. Startw with any
projection.
Let $w_1,\dots,w_{yvkatmeg}$ be the projections of $chzparwl_1,\dots,chzparwl_{yvkatmeg}$.
If the desired condition is not
achieved, we can choose $prxgmahu,vkyntewo$ such that
\[
|dfyqmsvo|^2 <  rac{1}{yvkatmeg} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{yvkatmeg}|^2) < |qzldnbpr|^2.
\]
By precomposing
with a suitable rotation that fixes $e_h$ for $h 
eq prxgmahu,vkyntewo$,
we can vary $|dfyqmsvo|, |qzldnbpr|$ without varying $|dfyqmsvo|^2 + |qzldnbpr|^2$
or $|klmroest|$ for $h 
eq prxgmahu,vkyntewo$. We can thus choose such a rotation to
force one of $|dfyqmsvo|^2, |qzldnbpr|^2$ to become equal to
$ rac{1}{yvkatmeg} (|w_1|^2 + \cdots + |w_{yvkatmeg}|^2)$.
Repeating at most $yvkatmeg-1$ times gives the desired configuration.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n,k\in\mathbb N$ with $1\le k<n$.  
For a matrix $A=[a_{ij}]\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times k}$ denote by  
$a_i=(a_{i1},\dots ,a_{ik})\in\mathbb R^{\,k}$ its $i$-th row and put  
\[
L_i(A)=\lVert a_i\rVert^{2}\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\]

The real Stiefel manifold is  
\[
\operatorname{St}(n,k)=\bigl\{A\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times k}\colon A^{\mathsf T}A=I_k\bigr\}.
\]

For $A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)$ let $\Gamma(A)$ be the graph with vertex
set $\{1,\dots ,n\}$ in which $i\neq j$ are adjacent iff
$\langle a_i,a_j\rangle\neq 0$.
Write $c(A)$ for the number of connected components of $\Gamma(A)$.

For a positive weight vector $w=(w_1,\dots ,w_n)$ satisfying  
$\sum_{i=1}^{n}w_i=k$ set  
\[
F_w=\Bigl\{A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)\colon
       L_1(A)=w_1,\dots ,L_n(A)=w_n\Bigr\},\qquad
F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}=\bigl\{A\in F_w\colon
       \Gamma(A)\text{ connected}\bigr\}.
\]

Further, for a multiset $L=(L_1,\dots ,L_n)$ of positive numbers define  
\[
S_L(r)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\min\{L_i,r^{2}\}}{r^{2}}
        \quad(r>0),\qquad
r_{\max}(L)=\sup\bigl\{r>0\colon S_L(r)\ge n\bigr\}.
\]

The problem has three independent parts.

1.  Basic properties of $S_L$.  
    (a)  Prove that, writing $L_{\min}:=\min_{1\le i\le n}L_i$,  
    \[
    S_L(r)=n\ \ (0<r\le\sqrt{L_{\min}}),\qquad
    S_L(r_1)>S_L(r_2)\ \ 
              (\sqrt{L_{\min}}<r_1<r_2),
    \]
    and deduce 
    \[
      \lim_{r\to\infty}S_L(r)=0,\qquad
      r_{\max}(L)=\sqrt{L_{\min}}.
    \]

    (b)  For
    \[
      \bigl(L_i\bigr)_{1\le i\le 12}=
        (4,4,4,\,2,2,2,2,\,1,1,1,1,1)
    \]
    compute $r_{\max}(L)$ exactly.

2.  Let $G\colon\operatorname{St}(n,k)\longrightarrow\mathbb R^{\,n}$,
    $G(A)=(L_1(A),\dots ,L_n(A))$.  
    Show that for every $A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)$  
    \[
        \operatorname{rank}\bigl(dG_A\bigr)=n-c(A).
    \]

3.  For the remainder fix $n=12$, $k=5$ and the uniform weight vector  
    \[
      w_1=\dots =w_{12}=\frac{5}{12}.
    \]

    (a)  Determine $r_{\max}(w)$.

    (b)  Prove that $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}\neq\varnothing$ and that the
    quotient manifold $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(k)$ has real
    dimension $24$.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use only the symbols $\times,\cdot,\in,\forall,\infty$ and
write every formula in correct \LaTeX.

------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Properties of $S_L$.

(a)  Constancy for small $r$.  
If $0<r\le\sqrt{L_{\min}}$ then $L_i\ge r^{2}$ for
all $i$, hence $\min\{L_i,r^{2}\}=r^{2}$ and
\[
S_L(r)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{r^{2}}{r^{2}}=n.
\]

Strict decrease for large $r$.  
Fix $\sqrt{L_{\min}}<r_1<r_2$.  For every index $i$ we have  
\[
\frac{\min\{L_i,r_1^{2}\}}{r_1^{2}}
      -\frac{\min\{L_i,r_2^{2}\}}{r_2^{2}}
=\begin{cases}
      \dfrac{L_i}{r_1^{2}}-\dfrac{L_i}{r_2^{2}}>0,
          &L_i<r_1^{2},\\[6pt]
      1-\dfrac{L_i}{r_2^{2}}\ge 0,
          &L_i\ge r_1^{2}.
  \end{cases}
\]
Because at least one $L_i$ satisfies $L_i<r_1^{2}$,
the sum of the $n$ non-negative differences is strictly positive:
$S_L(r_1)-S_L(r_2)>0$.

Limit at infinity.  
Choose $R>\sqrt{\max_i L_i}$.  
For $r\ge R$ we have $\min\{L_i,r^{2}\}=L_i$, whence  
\[
0\le S_L(r)=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}L_i}{r^{2}}
          \xrightarrow{\,r\to\infty\,}0.
\]
Consequently $S_L$ equals $n$ until $r=\sqrt{L_{\min}}$ and is already
below $n$ for every larger $r$; therefore  
$r_{\max}(L)=\sqrt{L_{\min}}$.

\smallskip
(b)  $\;L_{\min}=1$, hence $r_{\max}(L)=1$.

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Rank of $dG_A$.

Write  
\[
T_A\operatorname{St}(n,k)=
\bigl\{H\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times k}\colon A^{\mathsf T}H+H^{\mathsf T}A=0\bigr\}.
\]
For $H\in T_A\operatorname{St}(n,k)$
\[
dG_A(H)=2\bigl(\langle a_1,h_1\rangle,\dots ,
               \langle a_n,h_n\rangle\bigr)\in\mathbb R^{\,n}.
\]
Put $s_i(H)=\langle a_i,h_i\rangle$.

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  A \emph{lower} bound for $\dim\ker dG_A$.

Fix a non-zero skew-symmetric matrix $S\in\mathbb R^{\,k\times k}$
(so $S^{\mathsf T}=-S$).  
For every connected component $C$ of $\Gamma(A)$ define a matrix
$H^{(C)}=[h^{(C)}_{ij}]$ by
\[
h^{(C)}_i=
\begin{cases}
a_i\,S, & i\in C,\\[4pt]
0, & i\notin C.
\end{cases}
\]
Because $x^{\mathsf T}Sx=0$ for all $x\in\mathbb R^{\,k}$, we have
$\langle a_i,h^{(C)}_i\rangle=\langle a_i,a_iS\rangle=0$; hence
$dG_A\bigl(H^{(C)}\bigr)=0$.  On the other hand
\[
A^{\mathsf T}H^{(C)}=S\sum_{i\in C}L_i(A),\qquad
H^{(C)\mathsf T}A=-S\sum_{i\in C}L_i(A),
\]
so $A^{\mathsf T}H^{(C)}+H^{(C)\mathsf T}A=0$, i.e.\
$H^{(C)}\in T_A\operatorname{St}(n,k)$.
Different components have disjoint supports, hence the
$H^{(C)}$ are linearly independent.  Therefore
\[
\dim\ker dG_A\ge c(A)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)\le n-c(A).
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.2  A \emph{lower} bound for $\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)$.

For every edge $e=(p,q)$ of $\Gamma(A)$ define  
\[
H^{(e)}\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times k}
\quad\text{by}\quad
h^{(e)}_p=a_q,\;h^{(e)}_q=-a_p,\;
h^{(e)}_t=0\;(t\neq p,q).
\]
Exactly as before $H^{(e)}\in T_A\operatorname{St}(n,k)$ and
\[
dG_A\bigl(H^{(e)}\bigr)
   =2\langle a_p,a_q\rangle(e_p-e_q)\neq 0.
\]

Fix a component $C$.
The vectors $dG_A\bigl(H^{(e)}\bigr)$, $e$ edge of $C$,
span the codimension-$1$ subspace  
$\{x\in\mathbb R^{\,C}\colon\sum_{i\in C}x_i=0\}$.
Summing over all components gives  
$\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)\ge n-c(A)$.

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.3  Equality.

The inequalities of 2.1 and 2.2 yield  
\[
n-c(A)\le\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)\le n-c(A),
\]
hence
\[
\boxed{\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)=n-c(A).}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The case $n=12$, $k=5$ and $w_i=5/12$.

------------------------------------------------------------------
3(a)  The maximal radius.

All $w_i$ coincide, so by Part 1  
\[
\boxed{r_{\max}(w)=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{12}}.}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
3(b)  Existence of a connected configuration and the dimension count.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Construction of a matrix in $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$.

Because $12\equiv0\pmod4$, there exists a real $12\times12$
Hadamard matrix $H$ whose entries are $\pm1$ and whose rows are pairwise
orthogonal.  Set  
\[
O=\frac1{\sqrt{12}}\,H,\qquad
A=\text{first $5$ columns of }O.
\]
Then $O$ is orthogonal, so its first five columns are orthonormal;
hence $A\in\operatorname{St}(12,5)$.

Every entry of $A$ has magnitude $1/\sqrt{12}$; therefore each row of
$A$ has squared norm $5\cdot(1/12)=5/12$, i.e.\ $A\in F_w$.

In $H$ any two different rows agree in $6$ positions and disagree in
$6$; restricting to any $5$ columns produces an odd difference, hence  
$\langle a_r,a_s\rangle\neq0$ for $r\neq s$.  Thus
$\Gamma(A)$ is the complete graph and $A\in F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Smooth structure and dimension of $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$.

For every $A\in F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$ one has $c(A)=1$, so
by Part 2
\[
\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)=n-c(A)=12-1=11.
\]
The constant-rank theorem implies that
$F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$ is a smooth submanifold of
$\operatorname{St}(12,5)$ of codimension $11$.

The dimension of the Stiefel manifold is
\[
\dim\operatorname{St}(12,5)=12\cdot5-\frac12\cdot5\cdot6
                           =60-15=45,
\]
hence
\[
\dim F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}=45-11=34.
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Quotient by the orthogonal group.

The free right action $A\mapsto AQ$ for $Q\in\mathrm O(5)$ preserves
$F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$.  Because $\dim\mathrm O(5)=\tfrac12\cdot5\cdot4=10$,
the quotient is a smooth manifold of dimension
\[
\boxed{\dim\bigl(F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(5)\bigr)=34-10=24.}
\]

\bigskip
Summary for Part 3:
\[
r_{\max}(w)=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{12}},\qquad
\dim\bigl(F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(5)\bigr)=24.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher dimension and rectangular geometry.  
 The original problem dealt with a 2-ball inside a 4-cube; the present
 variant treats a k-ball inside an n-box with unequal side–lengths
 (and asks for both the general formula and a concrete 12×5 instance).

•  Non-linear optimisation.  
 Instead of a single quadratic inequality, one must solve the
 piece-wise quadratic non-linear equation (†) and understand its unique
 root; an explicit computation is required for the given data.

•  Frame theory and majorisation.  
 Showing sufficiency of (★) forces the solver to invoke deep facts about
 Parseval frames (or the Kadison–Singer/Carpenter theorem) rather than a
 simple Cauchy–Schwarz bound.

•  Classification of extremals.  
 The problem asks not only for the maximal radius but also for the
 complete description of all embeddings that attain it, a step that is
 absent in the original setting and requires an understanding of tight
 frames up to orthogonal equivalence.

•  Translation invariance.  
 Part (c) demands an argument that the optimum is unaffected by moving
 the centre, adding an extra conceptual layer.

•  Concrete high-dimensional construction.  
 Producing an explicit 12×5 extremal matrix forces the solver to juggle
 norms of three different sizes and to weave them into a tight frame, a
 task much subtler than writing down two perpendicular diagonals in a
 cube.

In sum, the problem blends linear algebra, convex geometry, optimisation
and frame theory, far surpassing the technical demands of both the
original problem and the prior kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $n,k\in\mathbb N$ with $1\le k<n$.  
For a matrix $A=[a_{ij}]\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times k}$ write $a_i\in\mathbb R^{\,k}$ for its $i$-th row and set  
\[
L_i(A)=\lVert a_i\rVert^{2},\qquad 1\le i\le n .
\]

Write  
\[
\operatorname{St}(n,k)=\bigl\{A\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times k}\colon A^{\mathsf T}A=I_k\bigr\}
\]
for the real Stiefel manifold, and for $A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)$ let
$\Gamma (A)$ be the graph whose vertex set is $\{1,\dots ,n\}$ and in which
$i\neq j$ are adjacent iff $\langle a_i,a_j\rangle\neq 0$.
Given a positive weight vector $w=(w_1,\dots ,w_n)$ with
$\sum_{i=1}^{n}w_i=k$, define  
\[
F_w=\Bigl\{A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)\colon L_1(A)=w_1,\dots ,L_n(A)=w_n\Bigr\},
\qquad
F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}=\bigl\{A\in F_w\colon\Gamma(A)\text{ connected}\bigr\}.
\]

For a multiset $L=(L_1,\dots ,L_n)$ of positive numbers put  
\[
S_L(r)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\min\{L_i,r^{2}\}}{r^{2}},\qquad r>0 ,
\quad\text{and}\quad
r_{\max}(L)=\sup\bigl\{r>0 \colon S_L(r)\ge n\bigr\}.
\]

The problem is divided into three independent parts.

1.  Basic properties of $S_L$.  
    (a)  Prove that
    \[
       S_L(r)=n\quad\text{for }0<r\le\sqrt{L_{\min}},\qquad 
       L_{\min}:=\min_{1\le i\le n}L_i ,
    \]
    and that $S_L$ is \emph{strictly decreasing} on
    $(\sqrt{L_{\min}},\infty)$ --- that is, whenever 
    $\sqrt{L_{\min}}<r_1<r_2$ one has $S_L(r_1)>S_L(r_2)$.  
    Deduce $\displaystyle\lim_{r\to\infty}S_L(r)=0$ and
    \[
        r_{\max}(L)=\sqrt{L_{\min}} .
    \]

    (b)  For
    $\bigl(L_i\bigr)_{1\le i\le 12}=(4,4,4,\,2,2,2,2,\,1,1,1,1,1)$
    compute $r_{\max}(L)$ exactly.

2.  Let $G\colon\operatorname{St}(n,k)\longrightarrow\mathbb R^{\,n}$ be the
    smooth map $G(A)=(L_1(A),\dots ,L_n(A))$.  
    Denote by $c(A)$ the number of connected components of $\Gamma(A)$.
    Prove that for every $A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)$  
    \[
       \operatorname{rank}\bigl(dG_A\bigr)=n-c(A).
    \]

3.  Fix $n=12$, $k=5$ and the weight vector  
\[
w_1=w_2=w_3=1,\qquad
w_4=w_5=w_6=w_7=\tfrac{8}{21},\qquad
w_8=w_9=w_{10}=w_{11}=w_{12}=\tfrac{2}{21}.
\]

    (a)  Determine $r_{\max}(w)$.

    (b)  Prove that $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}\neq\varnothing$ and that the
    quotient space $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(k)$ is a smooth
    manifold of real dimension $24$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we only use the symbols $\times,\cdot,\in,\forall,\infty$ and every
formula is written in proper \LaTeX.

\bigskip
\noindent\textbf{1(a) Behaviour of $S_L$.}

Let $L_{\min}=\min_i L_i$.

\emph{Plateau for small $r$.}
If $0<r\le\sqrt{L_{\min}}$ then $\min\{L_i,r^{2}\}=r^{2}$ for every
$i$, hence $S_L(r)=n$.

\emph{Strict decrease for $r>\sqrt{L_{\min}}$.}
Fix $r>\sqrt{L_{\min}}$ and set
$I(r)=\bigl\{i\colon L_i<r^{2}\bigr\}$.  
On an interval that contains $r$ but avoids the finitely many points $\sqrt{L_i}$ the set $I(r)$ is constant, so
\[
   S_L(r)=\sum_{i\in I(r)}\frac{L_i}{r^{2}}+
          \sum_{i\notin I(r)}1 .
\]
Differentiating inside that interval gives
\[
   S_L'(r)=
      -\frac{2}{r^{3}}\sum_{i\in I(r)}L_i<0,
\]
because $I(r)\neq\varnothing$.
Hence $S_L$ is strictly decreasing on each such open interval.
At a breakpoint $r_0=\sqrt{L_j}$ the function is continuous,
but $S_L'(r_0^{+})<0$, so the overall monotonicity is still strict:
for any $\sqrt{L_{\min}}<r_1<r_2$ one finds $S_L(r_1)>S_L(r_2)$.

\emph{Limit at infinity.}
Since $\min\{L_i,r^{2}\}\le r^{2}$,
\[
   0<S_L(r)\le\frac{n}{r^{2}}\longrightarrow 0
   \quad(r\to\infty).
\]

\emph{Maximal admissible radius.}
Because $S_L(r)=n$ on $(0,\sqrt{L_{\min}}]$ and $S_L(r)<n$ for every
$r>\sqrt{L_{\min}}$, the set $\{r>0\colon S_L(r)\ge n\}$ equals
$(0,\sqrt{L_{\min}}]$.  Thus
\[
   r_{\max}(L)=\sqrt{L_{\min}} .
\]

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{1(b) The concrete multiset.}

Here $L_{\min}=1$, hence  
\[
   r_{\max}(L)=1.
\]

\bigskip
\noindent\textbf{2 Rank of $dG_A$.}

Fix $A\in\operatorname{St}(n,k)$ and
let $H\in T_A\operatorname{St}(n,k)$, so
$A^{\mathsf T}H+H^{\mathsf T}A=0$.
Set
\[
   x:=dG_A(H)\in\mathbb R^{\,n},
   \qquad x_i=2\langle a_i,h_i\rangle .
\]

\emph{Step 1: ${\operatorname{Im}(dG_A)\subseteq W}$, where}
\[
   W=\Bigl\{y\in\mathbb R^{\,n}\colon
          \sum_{i\in C}y_i=0
          \ \text{for every component }C\text{ of }\Gamma(A)\Bigr\}.
\]
Let $C$ be a component and let $V_C=\operatorname{diag}(v)$ with
$v_i=1$ for $i\in C$ and $v_i=0$ otherwise.
Define
\[
   M_C:=A^{\mathsf T}V_CH-H^{\mathsf T}V_CA .
\]
Because $V_C$ is diagonal, $M_C^{\mathsf T}=-M_C$; hence
$\operatorname{tr}(M_C)=0$.  On the other hand
\[
   \operatorname{tr}(M_C)
   =2\sum_{i\in C}\langle a_i,h_i\rangle
   =\sum_{i\in C}x_i ,
\]
so $x\in W$.

\emph{Step 2: Surjectivity onto $W$.}
Choose, for each connected component $C$ of $\Gamma(A)$, a spanning
tree $T_C$.  Denote by $\mathcal E_C$ the edge set of $T_C$ and set
$\mathcal E=\bigcup_C \mathcal E_C$.  For each $\{i,j\}\in\mathcal E$
orient the edge from $j$ to $i$ if $y_i>0>y_j$ and from $i$ to $j$
otherwise; write the chosen orientation as $(p(e),q(e))$.

For every oriented edge $e=(p,q)\in\mathcal E$ define the matrix
\[
   H^{(e)}:=e_{p}a_{q}^{\mathsf T}-e_{q}a_{p}^{\mathsf T}\in
            \mathbb R^{\,n\times k},
\]
where $e_{p}$ is the $p$-th standard basis vector of $\mathbb R^{\,n}$.
One checks directly that
$A^{\mathsf T}H^{(e)}
     +{H^{(e)}}^{\mathsf T}A=0$,
so $H^{(e)}\in T_A\operatorname{St}(n,k)$, and
\[
   dG_A\bigl(H^{(e)}\bigr)
        =2\langle a_{p},a_{q}\rangle
          \bigl(e_{p}-e_{q}\bigr).
\]
Because $\langle a_{p},a_{q}\rangle\neq 0$ on every edge,
the vectors $dG_A(H^{(e)})$
with $e\in\mathcal E$ are linearly independent and there are
\[
\sum_{C}\bigl(\lvert C\rvert-1\bigr)=n-c(A)
\]
of them.
They span $W$ (standard ``flow-decomposition'' along the trees).
Consequently $dG_A$ is surjective onto $W$.

\emph{Step 3: Rank computation.}
Since ${\operatorname{Im}(dG_A)=W}$ and
$\dim W=n-c(A)$, we obtain
\[
   \operatorname{rank}\bigl(dG_A\bigr)=n-c(A).
\]

\bigskip
\noindent\textbf{3(a) The maximal radius for the chosen weights.}

With $\displaystyle\min_{1\le i\le 12}w_i=\tfrac{2}{21}$, Part~1 gives  
\[
   r_{\max}(w)=\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{21}} .
\]

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{3(b) Non-emptiness of $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$ and
smoothness of the quotient.}

\emph{Step 1: $F_w$ has positive dimension.}
The finite-dimensional Schur-Horn theorem ensures the existence of a rank-$5$ orthogonal projection $P$ on $\mathbb R^{\,12}$ with diagonal $(w_1,\dots ,w_{12})$.  
Pick an ordered orthonormal basis $\bigl(u_1,\dots ,u_5\bigr)$ for $\operatorname{Im}P$ and set  
\[
A:=[\,u_1\ \dots\ u_5\,]\in\operatorname{St}(12,5).
\]
Right-multiplying $A$ by any $Q\in\mathrm O(5)$ keeps $AA^{\mathsf T}=P$, hence keeps the diagonal $(w_i)$.  Therefore
\[
\Psi\colon\mathrm O(5)\longrightarrow F_w,\qquad Q\mapsto AQ ,
\]
is an injective smooth map.  Since $\dim\mathrm O(5)=10$, the image $\Psi(\mathrm O(5))$ is a $10$-dimensional submanifold of $F_w$.  Thus every connected component of $F_w$ is positive-dimensional.

\emph{Step 2: Existence of an $A\in F_w$ with $\Gamma(A)$ connected.}
For $1\le i<j\le 12$ the map
\[
    \Phi_{ij}\colon F_w\longrightarrow\mathbb R,\qquad
    \Phi_{ij}(B)=\langle b_i,b_j\rangle ,
\]
is real-analytic.
Its zero set $\Phi_{ij}^{-1}(0)$ is a proper real-analytic subset of
$F_w$, hence of codimension $1$.
A finite union of such codimension-$1$ subsets cannot cover a positive-dimensional real-analytic manifold, so there exists $A_0\in F_w$ outside $\bigcup_{i<j}\Phi_{ij}^{-1}(0)$.  For this $A_0$ we have $\langle a_{0i},a_{0j}\rangle\neq 0$ for all $i\neq j$; hence $\Gamma(A_0)$ is complete and therefore connected.  Thus $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}\neq\varnothing$.

\emph{Step 3: Smooth-manifold structure of $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$.}
For every $A\in F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$ one has $c(A)=1$, so
Part~2 gives $\operatorname{rank}(dG_A)=12-1=11$.
Hence the rank of $dG$ is constant and equal to $11$ along $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$.  
By the constant-rank theorem $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$
is a smooth submanifold of $\operatorname{St}(12,5)$ of codimension
$11$.  Using 
\[
   \dim\operatorname{St}(n,k)=nk-\frac12 k(k+1)
\]
we obtain
\[
   \dim F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}
      =(12\cdot 5)-\frac12\cdot5\cdot6-11
      =60-15-11
      =34 .
\]

\emph{Step 4: Passing to the quotient by $\mathrm O(5)$.}
Right multiplication by $\mathrm O(5)$ leaves every $L_i$ unchanged,
acts freely and properly on $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}$, and the
orbit space is the quotient
\[
   F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(5).
\]
Because $\dim\mathrm O(5)=\tfrac12\cdot5\cdot4=10$, it follows that
\[
   \dim\bigl(F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(5)\bigr)
   =34-10=24 .
\]
Therefore $F_w^{\operatorname{conn}}/\mathrm O(5)$ is a smooth
$24$-dimensional manifold.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher dimension and rectangular geometry.  
 The original problem dealt with a 2-ball inside a 4-cube; the present
 variant treats a k-ball inside an n-box with unequal side–lengths
 (and asks for both the general formula and a concrete 12×5 instance).

•  Non-linear optimisation.  
 Instead of a single quadratic inequality, one must solve the
 piece-wise quadratic non-linear equation (†) and understand its unique
 root; an explicit computation is required for the given data.

•  Frame theory and majorisation.  
 Showing sufficiency of (★) forces the solver to invoke deep facts about
 Parseval frames (or the Kadison–Singer/Carpenter theorem) rather than a
 simple Cauchy–Schwarz bound.

•  Classification of extremals.  
 The problem asks not only for the maximal radius but also for the
 complete description of all embeddings that attain it, a step that is
 absent in the original setting and requires an understanding of tight
 frames up to orthogonal equivalence.

•  Translation invariance.  
 Part (c) demands an argument that the optimum is unaffected by moving
 the centre, adding an extra conceptual layer.

•  Concrete high-dimensional construction.  
 Producing an explicit 12×5 extremal matrix forces the solver to juggle
 norms of three different sizes and to weave them into a tight frame, a
 task much subtler than writing down two perpendicular diagonals in a
 cube.

In sum, the problem blends linear algebra, convex geometry, optimisation
and frame theory, far surpassing the technical demands of both the
original problem and the prior kernel variant.
